title: Engineering Services

narrative.executive_view: Engineering Services is a credible but qualified AI value-migration target. Repetitive production, proposal, document-control, scheduling and review work can be compressed, while professional judgment, responsible charge and site-facing work remain human. The investable question is capture: public cost-accounting and competitive fixed-fee work can return part of the efficiency to clients. I would prefer a diversified, second-tier-led firm with recurring private/on-call relationships over a principal-dependent shop. [S1][S2][S4]
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: AI is already being used across operations and technical disciplines, including proposal writing, document search, code-compliance review, generative design and inspection support [S1]. It should first reduce hours in drafting, data reduction, report assembly, submittals, RFI handling, scheduling and internal knowledge retrieval. It does not replace the engineer of record, liability-bearing review, client/agency negotiation, physical observation or the judgment required to validate a model in a project-specific context. The consequence is capacity expansion before headcount elimination, consistent with surveyed firms expecting additional output at stable staffing. [S1]
narrative.value_capture: I assign only partial distribution retention because government A/E work is exposed to negotiated indirect-cost rates and reimbursable-cost procedures: FAR explicitly uses cost principles for A/E contracts and indirect-rate negotiation [S2], while FHWA maintains AASHTO audit guidance for consultant indirect-cost rates [S3]. This does not prove every public contract claws back AI savings, nor quantify public exposure; it does establish a real pass-through channel. In lump-sum and relationship-led work, a firm can retain some savings, but competitive bids and clients buying outcomes rather than hours limit the moat. [S2][S3]
narrative.adoption_timing: Current effective adoption is below the 50% threshold used here. ACEC reports 29% of surveyed member firms already had an AI strategy and another 34% were developing one in January 2025; strategy is a deliberately conservative proxy for operational deployment, not proof that a majority of tasks are transformed [S1]. Its companion evidence describes use as uneven and technical use as materially behind marketing/sales. I therefore use four years to 50% effective adoption, inside the report's three-to-five-year expectation window, rather than treating enthusiasm as realized automation. [S1]
narrative.consolidation_economics: Buyability is real: ACEC's 2025 review identified 13 member firms that made multiple acquisitions in a May–September window, with eight of the 13 PE-backed, and documented active demand in transportation, site-development, MEP and water [S4]. Valuation evidence is less clean than the deal pace: a 2026 buy-side guide reports 3.5–5.5x EBITDA for $1–3M EBITDA firms and 6–9x for platform-grade $3M-plus firms, but it aggregates A/E and relies partly on published survey anchors and advisor data [S6]. I use a conservative 4.5x buy and 6.0x exit contingent on normalized EBITDA after market-rate principal pay, not SDE or owner bonuses. [S6][S7]
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is DURABLE rather than immune. Construction-related A/E contracts remain subject to cost principles, and buyers specifically diligence who seals work, registrations, client relationships and transferable prequalifications [S2][S6]. Those requirements preserve demand for accountable firms and senior licensed judgment. Yet the billable production layer is pressured: clients can demand faster delivery and lower fees, and productivity gains may shrink a time-and-materials base. The durable asset is trusted responsibility, repeat relationships and integrated delivery—not a permanent claim on every drawing hour.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: This is a heterogeneous NAICS: civil/transportation, MEP, water, structural, energy and field-heavy specialties differ sharply in public exposure, field content and pricing. ACEC/BST samples are member-oriented and include broader engineering/design services, so neither the strategy percentage nor task expectations is a NAICS-541330 LMM census [S1]. The ownership-age input is an informed AEC proxy because the accessible Zweig release describes transition benchmarking but does not publish a 60-plus percentage [S5]. Multiples are also sensitive to backlog, principal dependence, client mix and whether reported earnings are SDE, pre-bonus profit or normalized EBITDA. [S5][S6]

sources.title: The Role of Artificial Intelligence in the Engineering Industry
sources.what_it_supports: ACEC's 2025 member/industry evidence on AI strategy, use cases, expected task automation, staffing expectations, uneven deployment and 3–5 year transformation expectations.

sources.title: FAR 31.105 Construction and architect-engineer contracts
sources.what_it_supports: Federal A/E cost principles, reimbursable-cost and indirect-rate negotiation mechanisms relevant to savings pass-through on applicable contracts.

sources.title: FHWA Consultant Services - Design
sources.what_it_supports: FHWA's current reference to the AASHTO Uniform Audit & Accounting Guide and training for A/E consultant indirect-cost-rate development and oversight.

sources.title: Headline: Big Buyers, Sellers Reshape Industry M&A
sources.what_it_supports: 2025 ACEC/Morrissey Goodale reporting on deal pace, repeat acquirers, PE participation and A/E specialty demand.

sources.title: Zweig Group releases 2026 Principals, Partners + Owners Report of AEC Firms
sources.what_it_supports: Existence and scope of a current AEC principal/ownership-transition benchmark, and reported ownership transition context; it does not state a 60-plus share.

sources.title: Architecture and Engineering Firm Valuation: What's Your A&E Firm Worth in 2026?
sources.what_it_supports: Directional LMM A/E normalized-EBITDA multiple ranges, size tiers, principal-dependence diligence and normalization cautions; third-party buy-side guide, not transaction-level primary data.

sources.title: 2025 Valuation Report of AEC Firms
sources.what_it_supports: Current AEC valuation survey scope, period and methodology; detailed multiples are paywalled and not directly used as numeric observations.

labor_share.method: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $172,759.1M / sectoral output (T30) $367,702.1M, year 2024
sources: BLS Industry Productivity and Costs flat files (ip.data.1.AllData), measures L02 and T30

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $260,281 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541330); class <5: 27,901 firms, avg revenue/firm $412,809 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $64,605 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 6,267 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,523,903 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $238,491 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 4,702 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,089,183 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $483,457 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 4,840 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,897,160 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,548,906 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 1,297 firms, avg revenue/firm $42,835,429 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,703,745 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 685 firms, avg revenue/firm $252,830,867 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $39,568,031 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 6,137
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Role-level estimate anchored to ACEC's reported use cases and expected task automation; technical deployment remains uneven and human review/field work are excluded.
caveats: ACEC/BST evidence spans engineering and design services, member firms and a small BST subsample rather than NAICS 541330 LMM employers.
Fractions measure replaceable task share, not adoption, pricing or terminal demand.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Calculations, model iterations, code checks and report drafting automate partly; responsible charge and project judgment remain human.
Schedules, budgets, submittals and RFI paperwork are exposed; coordination and claims judgment remain.
Analysis and documentation automate partly, while design responsibility and validation persist.
Reporting and resourcing administration automate, but client leadership and technical accountability persist.
Production drawings and document changes are strongly exposed, with supervised checking retained.
Analysis and documentation automate partly, while electrical design review and liability remain.
Internal reporting and routine coordination automate; operating leadership does not.
Coding is AI-augmented with human architecture, testing and security review retained.
Data reduction and drawing support automate, while physical testing and field context remain.
AI can assist documentation and prioritization, but site inspection requires presence and judgment.
Residual mixes specialty engineers, field staff and office support; conservative blend reflects heterogeneous exposure.

pi_dist.rationale: Applicable cost-based public A/E work and competitive bidding create a material pass-through channel, while fixed-fee and relationship work can retain some savings.
caveats: No source found quantifying 541330 revenue by public cost-based, fixed-fee or private contract type.
FAR/FHWA mechanisms apply to relevant government-assisted work, not every engineering engagement.

pi_moat.rationale: Licensure, responsible senior review, transferable client relationships and backlog can protect part of savings, but AI tools diffuse and principal dependence weakens transferability.
caveats: The cited valuation guide is third-party buy-side commentary and aggregated A/E evidence.

t50_years.rationale: ACEC reports uneven deployment and a three-to-five-year expectation window; four years is the midpoint judgment for 50% effective adoption.
caveats: The source reports expectations and strategy, not a longitudinal NAICS-541330 effective-adoption series.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: ACEC reports that 29% of surveyed member firms already had an AI strategy in January 2025.
caveats: This is AI-strategy prevalence, used as a conservative proxy for current effective adoption; it is not direct task-level adoption.
ACEC member-firm survey is broader than NAICS 541330 and may not represent LMM firms.

historical_analogs.value: CAD/BIM and prior design-software waves increased production capacity but retained accountable licensed review; this is a qualitative analogy, not a numeric forecast.
historical_analogs.rationale: ACEC situates current AI among engineering's long use of CAD, predictive modeling and other technology, supporting a capacity-first analogy.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Capacity expansion before headcount reduction; timing uncertain.
caveats: No quantified historical analog was selected; generative AI may diffuse faster than prior software waves.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: A cautious AEC ownership-transition proxy: current Zweig research covers principal ownership and transition but its accessible release does not publish age distribution.
caveats: No directly observed 60-plus percentage was available in the accessible source.
AEC scope includes architecture, construction and environmental consulting beyond 541330.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: Current AEC ownership-transition research and active external acquisitions indicate transition pressure and alternatives to internal succession, though neither accessible page quantifies a shortage.
caveats: This is a qualitative documentation judgment, not a measured shortage rate.

active_consolidators.rationale: ACEC identified 13 member firms making multiple acquisitions during May-September 2025.
caveats: Observed count is a short 2025 window and ACEC-member subset, not an all-market annual consolidator census.

buy_mult.rationale: Midpoint judgment within the reported 3.5-5.5x range for established $1-3M EBITDA A/E firms, conditioned on normalization of principal compensation.
caveats: The selected range is third-party guidance aggregating A/E and its underlying transaction data are not public.
Reported small-firm earnings may be SDE or pre-bonus profit; use market-rate owner compensation before applying EBITDA multiples.

exit_mult.rationale: A modest premium to entry assumes documented backlog, second-tier licensed leadership and strategic buyer interest; it is below the cited platform-grade upper range.
caveats: No transaction-level LMM 541330 exit-comparable set was available.
Exit multiple requires a larger, transferable platform and should not be assumed solely from sector deal volume.

pricing_structure.value: Mixed: negotiated/cost-based public A/E work with indirect-rate oversight, alongside fixed-fee and competitive private or on-call work.
pricing_structure.rationale: FAR and FHWA evidence establishes the cost-accounting channel for relevant public work; industry contract mix is otherwise heterogeneous.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Public cost-based exposure materially varies by specialty and geography.
caveats: No quantified NAICS-541330 contract-type split was found.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Yes. The estimated replaceable share is task-based and adoption remains below 50%; the four-year t50 reflects uneven implementation rather than using deal activity or licensure to accelerate adoption.

terminal_value.justification: Accountable licensed review, client relationships and construction/infrastructure delivery preserve paid demand, but billable production hours and fees are pressured; durability is not a capture claim. [S2][S6]

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No. AI evidence is broader ACEC/AEC member evidence and the 60-plus input is a disclosed AEC ownership-transition proxy; neither is presented as a 541330 LMM census.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Explicitly modeled in pi_dist: FAR cost principles, negotiated indirect rates and FHWA/AASHTO overhead-audit infrastructure can pass savings to clients on applicable public work. [S2][S3]

dataset_mismatch.description: No dataset fallback is used; deterministic labor, role and firm-count inputs are injected unchanged.

market_boundary.selection_basis: NAICS 541330 covers heterogeneous engineering services; broader AEC survey and valuation evidence is disclosed rather than treated as exact NAICS evidence.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Changes A directly; longer implementation lowers the score without changing the task-share estimate.
Public cost-based revenue mix and fixed-fee competition can materially change retained savings and C.
Moves B; accessible AEC research does not provide a direct age statistic.
Moves M; multiple expansion is contingent on transferability and platform quality.

reviewer_note: Research packet uses real, reopened sources. Score-driving proxy, population and contract-mix limitations are disclosed at the affected inputs.

A.arithmetic: Python v3.1.2: A = 10 when t50=0 else min(10,10/t50); t50=4, A=2.5
